title: It's War! (Part 1)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: It’s War! - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 6_10-13.txt

outline.title: Ephesians Culminates in a Call to War
subpoints: The main idea of Ephesians is the church — chapters 1-3 trace its origin in God's eternal purpose and its historical realization in salvation
Chapter 4 onward applies that rich theology to the life of the church community: walk worthy of the calling you have received
The word 'finally' in verse 10 means 'to what remains' — Paul brings everything together; some scholars say the whole letter was written to arrive at verse 10
The Christian life is a life of spiritual war — if you are not aware of that, you are already losing the battle

outline.title: How to Become Strong in the Lord
subpoints: First way: through the power of the Holy Spirit who already indwells every believer — the Spirit is not a future gift but a present reality
The expression 'the power of His might' (or 'His mighty power') has appeared twice before in Ephesians, both times in Paul's prayers (1:19; 3:16)
Ephesians 1:19: the power at work in believers is the same power that raised Jesus from the dead — if resurrection power raised Christ, it can equip us for war
Second way: through union with Christ — we are strong in the Lord, not in ourselves; we fight the war from a position of union with the conquering King

outline.title: The Nature of the Enemy: Who Are We Fighting?
subpoints: The war is not against flesh and blood — our enemies are not other people, institutions, governments, or cultural movements
Principalities, powers, rulers of the darkness of this age, and spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places — these are ranks in Satan's army
Satan is a liar and murderer (John 8:44), an angel of light (2 Corinthians 11:14), and a roaring lion (1 Peter 5:8) — he is powerful, deceptive, and relentless
Satan's fall is described in Isaiah and Luke; he is a created being who rebelled — a fallen angel, not an equal opposite to God
Satan is not omnipresent, not omniscient, not omnipotent — he is a creature; but he has organized forces far beyond what we naturally perceive

outline.title: The Whole Armor of God: Why We Need It
subpoints: God commands us to put on the whole armor — not partial armor, not the armor we choose, but the full panoplia (Greek for whole armor)
The armor is God's armor, not our own human invention — we stand in the strength of what God has provided, not our own spiritual effort
The command to 'stand' appears twice in verses 11 and 13 — the primary posture of the Christian in spiritual war is standing firm, not advancing on our own initiative
Satan is a defeated foe — Christ defeated him at the cross and resurrection (Colossians 2:15) — and we fight from that victory, not toward it

summary:
Opening a two-part series on the whole armor of God from Ephesians 6:10-13, Pastor Tito Lyro argues that the entire book of Ephesians has been building toward this call to spiritual warfare. Chapters 1-3 trace the church's origin in God's eternal decree and the historical application of that decree in salvation; chapters 4-6 apply that theology to the life of the church community; and the word 'finally' in verse 10 signals Paul's bringing everything to a climax: be strong in the Lord, because it's war.

The sermon develops two answers to the question of how one becomes strong in the Lord. First, through the power of the Holy Spirit who already indwells every believer — the same mighty power that raised Jesus from the dead is the power at work in the church (Ephesians 1:19). Second, through union with Christ — we are strong not in ourselves but in the Lord, fighting the war from a position of union with the conquering King.

The enemy is then carefully characterized: we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, powers, rulers of the darkness of this age, and spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places. Satan is a liar, a murderer, an angel of light, and a roaring lion — powerful, deceptive, and relentless. But he is a creature, a fallen angel, not omnipresent or omnipotent. And he is a defeated foe: the cross and resurrection of Christ are the decisive victory (Colossians 2:15). We fight from that victory, not toward it. The sermon closes with the command to put on the whole armor of God — not partial armor, but the full panoplia — and to stand firm.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 6:10-13

If you're able, would you remain standing for the reading of God's Word? We're going to turn to Ephesians chapter 6. I'm going to read verses 10 through 13.

*Finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord and in the power of His might. Put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil. For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this age, against spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places. Therefore take up the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand.*

That last phrase in verse 12 included the word "panoplia" — that's the transliteration into English of a Greek word that just means "the whole armor." That's what the word panoplia means, in case you're wondering.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray.

Father, we pray that Your Spirit will be working mightily through the proclamation of Your word, that Your people might be brought before Your presence and into growing in Christ even as we believe in what is preached. We pray that You would work mightily in us to grow in You and enable us to fight the fight that You call us to fight. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Ephesians and the Church

The main idea that Paul has been developing in the book of Ephesians is the idea of the church. This is the church. In chapters 1 through 3, Paul speaks of the origin of the church in the mind of God in eternity past, and then he speaks about the historical application of that in the salvation of God's people. In chapter 4, Paul starts to teach how the plans of God and the salvation in Jesus Christ look like as we live in the community of the church of Jesus Christ. Chapter 4 begins by telling us to walk worthy of the calling that we have in Jesus Christ. The Holy Spirit through Paul exhorts us to walk worthy of the call with which we were called — in other words, we are to live life here that is equivalent to the life that we have in heaven. In chapter 2, Paul already told us that we are sitting in a heavenly place in Jesus Christ — now we are to walk as such as we live in this world.

From chapter 4 on, Paul is applying the rich theology of God's sovereign grace through Jesus Christ to the life of the church. Now he brings all that he has said together, and the letter culminates with the command to be strong in the Lord.

If you look at verse 10, you find the word "finally" there — which just means "to what remains." I'm bringing everything together. Some scholars think the whole of the book of Ephesians was written in order to arrive at verse 10, where we are told that we are to fight a war by being strong in the Lord. Everything that Paul has said so far is designed to equip us for this war. Everything that Paul has said so far is designed to strengthen the church in the Lord and prepare her for war.

And the life of the Christian is a life of spiritual war in the name of Jesus Christ. If you are not aware of that, you are already losing the battle.

## How Do I Become Strong in the Lord?

In order to do that — in order to be able to fight that war — the Christian must be strong in the Lord. So I want us to start today by asking: how? How do I become strong in the Lord? How do we as a church of Jesus Christ become strong in the Lord?

I think Paul gives us two answers to this question.

**First: we become strong in the Lord through the power of the Holy Spirit who dwells in us.**

Look at verse 10: *Be strong in the Lord and in the power of His might.* The phrase "the power of His might" just means "His mighty power." So we become strong as the Spirit works in us. And the Spirit already dwells in us — it's not something we look forward to in the future. It's already here. Every one of us who believes in Jesus Christ has the Spirit of God.

This expression — "the power of His might" or "His mighty power" — has already appeared in Ephesians at least twice. Both times it appears in prayers of Paul for the Ephesians. In chapter 1 verse 19, he says: *What is the exceeding greatness of His power toward us who believe, according to the working of His mighty power, which He worked in Christ when He raised Him from the dead and seated Him at His right hand in the heavenly places.* This power that is at work in us — what God has given to you — is the same power that raised Jesus from the dead. If that power can bring people back to life, it can equip you to fight the war that God has placed before you.

Paul had prayed for the Ephesians that they would be strong in the Lord through them. He prayed for us. In chapter 3 verse 16, Paul prays that the Father would grant to the Ephesians to be strengthened with might through his Spirit in the inner man.

**Second: we become strong in the Lord through union with Christ.**

We are strong not in ourselves, but in the Lord. Notice that Paul doesn't say: be strong — in your determination, in your spiritual effort, in the spiritual disciplines. He says: be strong in the Lord. And we are at our strongest when we are most empty of ourselves and filled with the Spirit of God — united to the conquering King, Jesus Christ. We conquer his and our enemies. And we fight the war from that perspective.

Satan is a defeated foe. He is fighting for his life. But you and I have been given life — and life more abundantly. So we can fight with courage, knowing that we have a powerful enemy, but also knowing that we have a more powerful King who has gone before us and is waiting for us.

## The Nature of the Enemy

Now, before we can put on any armor, we need to understand who it is we're fighting. Look at verse 12:

*For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this age, against spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places.*

This is one of the most important things to grasp in the Christian life: your enemy is not other people. Your enemy is not your neighbor. Your enemy is not that politician you don't like. Your enemy is not that difficult person at work. Your enemy is not the institution or the government that is doing things you disagree with. Your enemy is not a cultural movement. Your enemy is spiritual forces.

Principal principalities, powers, rulers of the darkness of this age, spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places — these are the ranks of Satan's army. We are fighting organized spiritual forces of evil that are working through the world and working against the church of Jesus Christ.

Now who is Satan? Our culture has two extremes in how it portrays Satan. The one is the red man in a red suit with a pitchfork and a little tail who is a caricature — so caricatured that nobody really believes he exists. The other extreme is that Satan is portrayed as equal to God — the dark side to God's light side, two equal and opposite forces. Neither of these is what the Bible teaches.

Satan is a created being — a fallen angel who rebelled against God. He was created good, as all of God's creatures were. He was likely one of the most glorious of the angels. And in his pride he rebelled. He is not omnipresent, not omniscient, not omnipotent — he is a creature. But he is a powerful creature who has organized forces of spiritual darkness far beyond what we naturally perceive.

Jesus says in John 8:44 that Satan is a liar and the father of lies — there is no truth in him; when he speaks, he speaks from his own character. And in John 10:10, Jesus says: *The thief does not come except to steal, and to kill, and to destroy.* That is Satan's mission.

Paul says in 2 Corinthians 11:14 that Satan disguises himself as an angel of light — he is deceptive. He does not always come as obvious evil. He often comes as something that looks good, something that looks attractive, something that appears to be true.

Peter says in 1 Peter 5:8: *Your adversary the devil walks about like a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour.* He is relentless — he prowls about, looking for the opportune moment.

But here is the key thing: Satan is a defeated foe. Christ defeated him at the cross and resurrection. As Paul says in Colossians 2:15: *Having disarmed principalities and powers, He made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in it.* The cross was not a defeat for Christ — it was a victory. Satan lost at the cross. He is fighting for his life, but the outcome has already been determined. And because we are united to the conquering King, we share in that victory.

Romans 8:37-39: *In all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor principalities nor powers, nor things present nor things to come, nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.*

## Why We Need the Whole Armor

So Paul commands us in verse 11: *Put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil.*

Notice several things about this command.

First: it's the whole armor. Not partial armor. Not the parts we prefer. Not the parts that are comfortable. The whole panoplia — the full set. A Roman soldier going into battle with only some of his armor was a Roman soldier who was about to die. The parts of the armor not covered are the parts the enemy will target.

Second: it's God's armor. We are putting on armor that God has provided, not armor that we have invented or manufactured by our own spiritual effort. We stand in the strength of what God has provided for us.

Third: the command is to stand. Twice in this passage — in verse 11 and in verse 13 — Paul says "stand." And at the end of verse 13: "having done all, to stand." The primary posture of the Christian in spiritual warfare is not aggressive advance — it is standing firm. Holding the ground that Christ has already won. Not being moved. Not being pushed back. Holding fast to what we already have in Christ.

Satan is a defeated foe. He is fighting for his life. And we fight the war from that perspective — united to the conquering King who has already secured the victory, and who is waiting for us to join him in the fullness of that victory.

Let us pray together. Father, you have shown us that you have given us the Lord Jesus Christ, and in his cross and resurrection he defeated his and our enemies. Enable us to live a life knowing that Christ is King and yet that we are still at war — that the end is declared as victory, but we are still fighting the battles of every day life. Protect us against the attacks of Satan, and give us the grace and the courage to fight him back. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
